business_ideas.idea: Cintas: uniform rental and facility service company ($9B revenue)
School of entrepreneurship: 6-12 boarding school teaching business skills

predictions.text: Market crashes are normal; most people overreact despite 30+ year gains in index funds

disagreements: Ben wants to change strategy on market crash; Shaan argues crisis doesn't justify pivoting from committed long-term priorities unless existential
dynamics: Sam leads market discussion; Shaan provides perspective on panic vs logic. Deep conversation about family businesses, parenting, Olympics, and priorities. Multiple tangents show how life philosophy affects business decisions.
frameworks: Reaction vs response: emotion-based immediate decision vs logical decision after cooling off
Three big shifts framework: identify annual priorities, reinforce weekly, stay focused despite noise
Is the wolf at the door: assess existential risk before changing strategy
Barbell relationships: many light touches plus occasional deep hangs
Set it and forget it: index funds beat 95% of active managers
Find people you love and do life with them: maximize joy through collaboration
Children of deadbeat dads grow up tough: independence via benign neglect
Generational wealth: family ownership requires training next generation
Opportunity cost: optimize for what matters most, accept tradeoffs
learnings: Market corrections normal and predictable; reacting causes losses, inaction gains
5% weekly drop in S&P recovers fully with 5-10 year horizon; worst days matter most
VTI (total market index): up 8-10% year-to-date despite crash; buy and hold beats market timing
If no existential crisis (wolf at door), maintain strategy for 30-90 days before reassessing
Over-leveraging or over-commitment creates false urgency to react to market moves
Past mistakes (COVID reaction, 2020 cash shift) show cost of over-reacting to headlines
K-shaped recovery: divergence between assets and labor; mainstream gets left behind
Successful families pass generational wealth through training, not abdication or over-control
Cintas shows durable model: started 1929 circus rag laundry, evolved into $75B company
Subscription revenue (uniform rental) more valuable than transactional sales

banger_quotes.quote: Is the wolf at the door? If not, don't react. Wait 30-90 days.
Taking a simple idea very seriously is powerful
Children of deadbeat dads grow up tough and independent
Find the people you love and do life with them
When they come at you this way, use your feet to protect the ball
You know why I got to do that? Dad is teaching me to be a big human
Stocks: short term measuring stick, long term weighing machine

numbers_dropped: 2024 market drop: 5% this week, S&P 500 down 5% YTD
Worst day: Dow worst day in 2 years
Tech stocks: Nvidia down 12% on correction
Cintas by 1968: $1.6M revenue (15M in today's dollars)
Cintas growth: 50+ years of scaling, family owns ~18-20%
Richard Farmer: took over age 21-23, ran for 50 years
Set and forget gains: $10k invested in 1990 = $181k if held; missed 30 best days = $31k; missed 10 best days = $83k
notable: Olympics discussion (Cole Hocker 1500m win, Femke Bol, refugee team, Noah Lyles) interwoven with market/business themes; water park parenting moment illustrates how parental presence and teaching create generational legacy better than money.
